stem:
A patient presents with inability to abduct the arm beyond 15 degrees following a shoulder injury. Which muscle is most likely damaged?

Which nerve supplies this muscle?
options: Deltoid — axillary nerve
Supraspinatus — suprascapular nerve
Infraspinatus — suprascapular nerve
Subscapularis — upper and lower subscapular nerves
Teres minor — axillary nerve
explanation: Supraspinatus initiates abduction from 0-15 degrees. It is supplied by the suprascapular nerve (C5, C6). After 15 degrees, deltoid (axillary nerve) takes over. The rotator cuff — SITS: Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis — stabilises the glenohumeral joint.

stem: Which bones form the proximal row of the carpus from lateral to medial?
options: Trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate
Scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform
Scaphoid, lunate, capitate, hamate
Trapezium, scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum
Pisiform, triquetrum, lunate, scaphoid
explanation: The proximal row lateral to medial: scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform. The distal row lateral to medial: trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate. The scaphoid and lunate articulate with the radius at the radiocarpal joint.

stem: What are the seven tarsal bones? Which articulates with the tibia and fibula to form the ankle joint?
answer: Talus / calcaneus / navicular / cuboid / medial cuneiform / intermediate cuneiform / lateral cuneiform. The talus articulates with the tibia and fibula to form the talocrural (ankle) joint.
explanation: The talus sits on top of the calcaneus. The subtalar joint between talus and calcaneus allows inversion and eversion.

stem: Which muscle group forms the floor of the anatomical snuffbox, and what important structure can be palpated here?
options: Extensor pollicis longus — radial nerve
Abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis — scaphoid and radial artery
Flexor pollicis longus — median nerve
Extensor carpi radialis longus — radial artery only
Opponens pollicis — scaphoid only
explanation: The floor of the anatomical snuffbox contains the scaphoid bone and the radial artery passing to the deep palmar arch. The borders are APL and EPB anteriorly and EPL posteriorly. Tenderness here after a fall suggests scaphoid fracture.

stem: Which nerve supplies the anterior compartment of the leg and what is its terminal sensory distribution?
options: Superficial fibular nerve — dorsum of the foot except first web space
Deep fibular nerve — first dorsal web space
Tibial nerve — sole of the foot
Sural nerve — lateral foot and small toe
Saphenous nerve — medial leg and foot
explanation: The deep fibular (peroneal) nerve supplies the anterior compartment of the leg: tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum longus, extensor hallucis longus, fibularis tertius. Its only sensory distribution is the first dorsal web space. The superficial fibular nerve supplies the lateral compartment and the dorsum of the foot.

stem: The popliteal artery divides below the knee into which vessels?
options: Femoral artery and posterior tibial artery
Anterior tibial artery and posterior tibial artery
Fibular artery and dorsalis pedis
Anterior tibial artery and fibular artery only
Posterior tibial artery and dorsalis pedis
explanation: The popliteal artery divides into the anterior tibial artery and posterior tibial artery below the knee. The fibular (peroneal) artery is a branch of the posterior tibial artery, supplying the lateral compartment via perforating vessels. The anterior tibial artery continues as the dorsalis pedis at the ankle.

stem: Describe the medial meniscus — its shape and why it is more commonly injured than the lateral meniscus.
answer: The medial meniscus is C-shaped and is firmly attached to the medial collateral ligament and the joint capsule, making it relatively immobile. The lateral meniscus is more circular and loosely attached so it can move to avoid injury. The medial meniscus cannot escape being trapped during rotational injuries.
explanation: Medial meniscus tears commonly occur with the O'Donoghue unhappy triad: ACL + MCL + medial meniscus, from a valgus rotational force on the knee.

stem: What is the profunda femoris and what does it supply?
options: A branch of the popliteal artery supplying the posterior leg
The largest branch of the femoral artery supplying the femur and all three thigh compartments
A branch of the internal iliac artery supplying the medial thigh
A branch of the external iliac supplying the femoral head
The continuation of the femoral artery through the adductor hiatus
explanation: The profunda femoris (deep artery of the thigh) is the largest branch of the femoral artery, arising posterolaterally ~4 cm below the inguinal ligament. It supplies all three thigh compartments via perforating branches and gives medial and lateral circumflex femoral arteries supplying the femoral head.

stem: A patient cannot extend their wrist or fingers following a mid-humeral fracture. Which nerve is injured and what is its relation to the humerus at this level?
options: Median nerve — passes anterior to the medial epicondyle
Ulnar nerve — passes posterior to the medial epicondyle
Radial nerve — runs in the spiral groove on the posterior surface of the humerus
Axillary nerve — winds around the surgical neck
Musculocutaneous nerve — pierces coracobrachialis
explanation: The radial nerve runs in the spiral (radial) groove on the posterior surface of the humerus, accompanied by the profunda brachii artery. Mid-shaft fractures damage the radial nerve causing wrist drop, finger drop, and loss of sensation over the posterior forearm and dorsal hand.

stem: The right subclavian artery arises from which vessel, and at what bony landmark does it become the axillary artery?
options: Directly from aortic arch — medial border of first rib
Brachiocephalic trunk — lateral border of first rib
Left common carotid — medial border of clavicle
Brachiocephalic trunk — inferior border of teres major
Directly from aortic arch — lateral border of first rib
explanation: The right subclavian artery arises from the brachiocephalic trunk (first branch of the aortic arch). It becomes the axillary artery as it crosses the lateral border of the first rib. The axillary artery then becomes the brachial artery at the inferior border of teres major.

stem: Name the boundaries and contents of the cubital fossa from lateral to medial.
answer: Lateral: brachioradialis. Medial: pronator teres. Superior: line between epicondyles. Contents lateral to medial: Radial nerve / Biceps tendon / Brachial artery / Median nerve — Really Boring But Nice.
explanation: The brachial artery bifurcates in the cubital fossa into the radial and ulnar arteries, separated by pronator teres.

stem: Erb's palsy affects the upper trunk (C5-C6) of the brachial plexus. What is the characteristic posture and which movements are lost?
options: Claw hand — loss of intrinsic hand muscles
Waiter's tip — loss of shoulder abduction, elbow flexion, forearm supination
Wrist drop — loss of wrist and finger extension
Foot drop equivalent — loss of shoulder elevation
Benediction hand — loss of median nerve function
explanation: Erb's palsy causes the waiter's tip posture: adducted arm, internally rotated, extended elbow, pronated forearm. Lost movements: shoulder abduction (deltoid/supraspinatus C5), elbow flexion (biceps C5/C6), forearm supination (biceps/supinator C6). Hand function is preserved (C8/T1 intact).

stem: The internal carotid artery feels swollen at its origin compared to the external carotid. Why?
options: It contains the carotid body — a chemoreceptor
It contains the carotid sinus — a baroreceptor for blood pressure monitoring
It has thicker walls due to higher systemic pressure
It contains a valve preventing backflow
It is larger because it supplies the brain
explanation: The carotid sinus is a dilation of the internal carotid artery at its origin. It is a baroreceptor that monitors blood pressure, with afferent signals via the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN9). Carotid sinus massage reduces heart rate via the baroreflex. The carotid body (chemoreceptor) is a small structure at the bifurcation.

stem: Which nerve supplies all four muscles of mastication?
options: Facial nerve (CN7) — temporal branch
Trigeminal nerve — ophthalmic branch (V1)
Trigeminal nerve — maxillary branch (V2)
Trigeminal nerve — mandibular branch (V3)
Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN9)
explanation: The mandibular branch (V3) of the trigeminal nerve supplies all four muscles of mastication: masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid. V3 is the only branch of the trigeminal with a motor component. The facial nerve (CN7) supplies all muscles of facial expression.

stem: Name the six main branches of CN7 (facial nerve) and within which gland do they arise?
answer: Posterior auricular / temporal / zygomatic / buccal / marginal mandibular / cervical. They arise within the parotid gland.
explanation: Mnemonic: Zaddy's Big Muscular Cock Tears Pussies. A peripheral CN7 lesion affects ALL ipsilateral facial muscles including forehead. A central lesion spares the forehead.

stem: What structures form the boundaries of the pelvic outlet?
options: Sacrum, ilium, pubis and pubic symphysis
Coccyx, sacrum, sacrotuberous ligament, ischium, pubis and pubic symphysis
Inguinal ligament, iliac crest, sacrum
Pelvic diaphragm, obturator membrane, sacrum
Ilium, ischium, pubis and coccyx only
explanation: The pelvic outlet is bounded by: coccyx and sacrum posteriorly / sacrotuberous ligament / ischial tuberosities / pubic arch and pubic symphysis anteriorly. The pelvic inlet is bounded by sacrum, ilium, pubis and pubic symphysis.

stem: Once an ovarian follicle releases its oocyte, what does it become and what hormone does it secrete?
options: Corpus albicans — oestrogen
Corpus luteum — progesterone
Graafian follicle — LH
Corpus luteum — oestrogen
Corpus albicans — progesterone
explanation: After ovulation the follicle transforms into the corpus luteum which secretes progesterone. Progesterone prepares the endometrium for implantation. If fertilisation does not occur the corpus luteum degenerates to the corpus albicans and progesterone falls, triggering menstruation.

stem: Name the layers of the pelvic floor from superficial to deep when making an incision in the urogenital triangle.
answer: Superficial fascia / superficial perineal muscles / perineal membrane / deep perineal muscles / external fascia / pelvic diaphragm / internal fascia.
explanation: The pelvic diaphragm (levator ani + coccygeus) forms the main muscular floor of the pelvis. The perineal membrane is a fibrous sheet forming the roof of the superficial perineal pouch.

stem: Which ribs are classified as true, false and floating?
options: True: 1-5 / False: 6-10 / Floating: 11-12
True: 1-7 / False: 8-10 / Floating: 11-12
True: 1-6 / False: 7-10 / Floating: 11-12
True: 1-8 / False: 9-10 / Floating: 11-12
True: 1-7 / False: 8-9 / Floating: 10-12
explanation: True ribs (1-7) articulate directly with the sternum via costal cartilage. False ribs (8-10) articulate indirectly via the costal cartilage of the rib above. Floating ribs (11-12) have no anterior articulation. The manubrium articulates with ribs 1 and part of rib 2. The sternal angle marks where rib 2 articulates with the sternum.

stem: Which accessory muscles of respiration are identified in the practical sheet?
options: Trapezius, rhomboids, levator scapulae, serratus anterior
Pectoralis major, serratus anterior, scalenus anterior, sternocleidomastoid
Pectoralis minor, intercostals, diaphragm, serratus posterior
Latissimus dorsi, trapezius, pectoralis major, SCM
External intercostals, internal intercostals, pectoralis major, SCM
explanation: The four accessory muscles of respiration from the S2P2 practical: pectoralis major / serratus anterior / scalenus anterior / sternocleidomastoid. Scalenus anterior attaches to the scalene tubercle of the first rib, with the subclavian vein anterior and subclavian artery posterior to it.

stem: Where do the anterior and posterior intercostal arteries originate from, and where do the anterior and posterior intercostal veins drain?
answer: Anterior intercostal arteries: from internal thoracic artery (upper spaces) or musculophrenic artery (lower spaces). Posterior intercostal arteries: from thoracic descending aorta. Anterior intercostal veins: drain into internal thoracic or musculophrenic veins. Posterior intercostal veins: drain into azygos system (right) or hemiazygos/accessory hemiazygos (left).
explanation: The internal thoracic artery originates from the subclavian artery and runs parasternally. It terminates as the superior epigastric and musculophrenic arteries.

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: At what vertebral level does the abdominal aorta bifurcate and what does it divide into?
options: L2 — left and right renal arteries
L3 — superior and inferior mesenteric arteries
L4 — left and right common iliac arteries
L5 — left and right external iliac arteries
T12 — coeliac trunk and SMA
explanation: The abdominal aorta bifurcates at L4 into the left and right common iliac arteries. Each common iliac then divides at the sacroiliac joint into internal iliac (pelvis) and external iliac (lower limb via femoral artery under inguinal ligament).

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: What are the contents of the carotid sheath?
options: Common carotid artery, subclavian vein, phrenic nerve
Common carotid artery, internal jugular vein, vagus nerve
Internal carotid artery, external jugular vein, glossopharyngeal nerve
Common carotid artery, internal jugular vein, hypoglossal nerve
External carotid artery, internal jugular vein, vagus nerve
explanation: The carotid sheath contains: common carotid artery (medial) / internal jugular vein (lateral) / vagus nerve CN10 (posterior between artery and vein). The common carotid gives no branches until its bifurcation at C4 into internal and external carotid arteries.

topic: Blood Supply to Head, Neck and Limbs
stem: Name the three branches of the aortic arch in order and state the different origins of the left and right common carotid arteries.
answer: 1st: brachiocephalic trunk (→ right subclavian + right common carotid). 2nd: left common carotid artery. 3rd: left subclavian artery. Right common carotid arises from the brachiocephalic trunk. Left common carotid arises directly from the arch of the aorta.
explanation: All three branches arise at the level of the sternal angle (T4/5). The sternal angle also marks the beginning and end of the aortic arch, the carina, and the azygos vein draining into the SVC.

stem: The superior mesenteric artery arises at which vertebral level and supplies which portion of the gut?
options: T12 — foregut including stomach and liver
L1 — midgut from distal duodenum to proximal two-thirds of transverse colon
L3 — hindgut from splenic flexure to upper rectum
L2 — gonadal organs and kidneys
L4 — sigmoid colon and rectum
explanation: The SMA arises at L1 and supplies the midgut: distal duodenum / jejunum / ileum / caecum / ascending colon / proximal two-thirds of transverse colon. Coeliac trunk (T12) = foregut. IMA (L3) = hindgut.

stem: If portal hypertension develops, which clinical signs would you expect at the sites of portocaval anastomosis?
options: Jaundice, pale stools, dark urine
Oesophageal varices, anorectal varices, caput medusae
Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites only
Haematemesis from gastric ulcers only
Bilateral leg oedema and ascites only
explanation: Portal hypertension causes reversal of flow through portocaval anastomoses: oesophageal varices (distal oesophagus — risk of fatal haemorrhage) / anorectal varices (rectum/anal canal) / caput medusae (peri-umbilical dilated veins resembling Medusa's snakes). The lack of valves in portal veins allows bidirectional flow.

stem: Name the three major midline branches of the abdominal aorta, the vertebral level each arises at, and what each supplies.
answer: Coeliac trunk (T12) — foregut (distal oesophagus to mid-duodenum, plus liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas). Superior mesenteric artery (L1) — midgut (distal duodenum to proximal 2/3 transverse colon). Inferior mesenteric artery (L3) — hindgut (distal 1/3 transverse colon to upper rectum).
explanation: The paired lateral branches: suprarenal arteries (L1) / renal arteries (L1) / gonadal arteries (L2). The aorta bifurcates at L4.

stem: What prevents the alveoli from collapsing between breaths?
options: Elastic recoil of the lung parenchyma
Continuous positive airway pressure from the trachea
Pulmonary surfactant coating the alveolar surface reducing surface tension
Cartilaginous rings maintaining alveolar patency
Active contraction of smooth muscle in the alveolar walls
explanation: Pulmonary surfactant is produced by type II pneumocytes and coats the alveolar surface, reducing surface tension and preventing collapse between breaths. Surfactant deficiency in premature neonates causes respiratory distress syndrome (RDS).

stem: The vagus nerve provides parasympathetic innervation to which thoracic structures, and what does it pass posterior to?
options: Heart only — passes posterior to the trachea
Lungs and heart — passes posterior to the root of the lung
Diaphragm — passes posterior to the oesophagus
Lungs only — passes posterior to the aorta
Heart and pericardium — passes anterior to the root of the lung
explanation: The vagus nerve provides parasympathetic innervation to both the lungs and heart in the thorax. It passes posterior to the root of the lung. The visceral pleura is innervated by the autonomic nervous system and is insensitive to pain.

stem: Name the structures that pass through the hilum of the lung.
answer: Main bronchus / pulmonary arteries / pulmonary veins. Also: bronchial arteries and veins / lymph vessels / efferent and afferent autonomic nerves.
explanation: The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium — the exception to the rule that veins carry deoxygenated blood. The pulmonary ligament stabilises the inferior lobe.

stem: At which part of the bronchial tree does the function change from air conduction to gas exchange?
options: Primary bronchi
Secondary (lobar) bronchi
Tertiary (segmental) bronchi
Terminal bronchioles
Respiratory bronchioles
explanation: Gas exchange begins at the respiratory bronchioles where alveolar outpouchings first appear in the walls. Before this level (trachea to terminal bronchioles) the airways are purely conducting — the anatomical dead space. Alveoli provide the main gas exchange surface.

stem: How do the right and left ventricles differ from one another and why?
options: Right has thicker walls — generates higher pressure for systemic circulation
Left has thicker walls — generates higher pressure for systemic circulation
Both have identical wall thickness — different only in valve type
Right has thicker walls — receives blood from pulmonary veins
Left has thinner walls — lower resistance pulmonary circulation
explanation: The left ventricle has thicker walls (approximately 3x) than the right because it generates much higher pressures for systemic circulation (120 mmHg) compared to the right ventricle which pumps to the low-resistance pulmonary circulation (25 mmHg). The left has a mitral (bicuspid) valve; the right has a tricuspid valve.

stem: The aortic valve is best auscultated at which location on the chest wall?
options: 5th intercostal space, left midclavicular line
2nd intercostal space, right sternal edge
2nd intercostal space, left sternal edge
4th intercostal space, left sternal edge
3rd intercostal space, right sternal edge
explanation: Auscultation areas: Aortic — 2nd ICS right sternal edge / Pulmonary — 2nd ICS left sternal edge / Tricuspid — 4th-5th ICS left sternal edge / Mitral — 5th ICS midclavicular line (apex). These are where the blood sounds travel to after passing through each valve, not the anatomical position of the valves.

stem: Name the contents of each division of the mediastinum.
answer: Superior: arch of aorta / brachiocephalic veins / SVC / trachea / oesophagus / thoracic duct / thymus / vagus and phrenic nerves. Anterior: thymus (remnant). Middle: heart / pericardium / origins of great vessels / tracheal bifurcation / main bronchi / phrenic nerves. Posterior: thoracic aorta / azygos and hemiazygos veins / oesophagus / thoracic duct / vagus nerves / sympathetic chains.
explanation: The superior and inferior mediastina are divided by the sternal angle (T4/5). The middle mediastinum is separated from anterior and posterior by the pericardium.

stem: The transverse pericardial sinus lies between which structures and why is it surgically important?
options: Between aorta and SVC — posteriorly — allows ligation of great vessels during cardiac surgery
Between pulmonary veins and IVC — allows drainage of pericardial effusion
Between right and left atria — allows access to atrial septum
Between pericardium and diaphragm — allows pericardiocentesis
Between aorta and pulmonary trunk — allows access to coronary arteries
explanation: The transverse pericardial sinus lies posterior to the aorta and pulmonary trunk, anterior to the SVC. It is surgically important because a ligature can be passed through it to gain vascular control of the aorta and pulmonary trunk prior to cardiopulmonary bypass (CABG).

stem: What structure forms the boundary between the nasopharynx and oropharynx?
options: Hard palate
Soft palate
Upper margin of the epiglottis
Palatine tonsils
Posterior wall of the pharynx
explanation: The soft palate forms the boundary between the nasopharynx and oropharynx. The upper margin of the epiglottis forms the boundary between the oropharynx and laryngopharynx. The laryngopharynx is continuous inferiorly with the trachea and oesophagus.

stem: What is the eustachian tube and why are people encouraged to yawn to pop their ears?
options: A drainage tube from the maxillary sinus — yawning opens it to drain mucus
The auditory tube connecting the nasopharynx to the middle ear — yawning opens it allowing pressure equalisation
A lymphatic vessel draining the tonsils — yawning clears lymph
A nerve canal in the temporal bone — yawning stretches it
A connection between the oropharynx and inner ear — yawning allows sound transmission
explanation: The eustachian (auditory) tube connects the nasopharynx to the middle ear via the lateral wall of the nasopharynx. Yawning opens its pharyngeal opening allowing pressure equalisation between the middle ear and atmosphere. Blockage causes the sensation of blocked ears during altitude change or infection.

stem: Name the three unpaired cartilages of the larynx. Which is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the airway and what is its clinical significance?
answer: Thyroid cartilage / cricoid cartilage / epiglottis. The cricoid cartilage is the only complete ring in the airway. Clinical significance: (1) in children it is the narrowest part of the airway — foreign bodies lodge here; (2) the cricothyroid membrane between thyroid and cricoid is the site of emergency cricothyroidotomy in cannot intubate cannot oxygenate scenarios.
explanation: The tracheal rings are C-shaped (incomplete posteriorly, completed by the trachealis muscle). The arytenoid cartilages are paired and anchor the vocal cords posteriorly.

stem: Which nerves provide motor innervation to the pharynx?
options: Hypoglossal nerve and glossopharyngeal nerve
Vagus nerve and glossopharyngeal nerve
Facial nerve and vagus nerve
Accessory nerve only
Trigeminal nerve mandibular branch
explanation: The pharynx receives motor innervation from the pharyngeal branches of the vagus nerve (carrying the cranial root of the accessory nerve) and the glossopharyngeal nerve. The longitudinal muscles elevate the pharynx. The constrictor muscles reduce the luminal size during swallowing.

stem: Which feature uniquely identifies a thoracic vertebra?
options: Transverse foramina and bifid spinous process
Large bean-shaped body with interlocking articular facets
Costal facets on the body and transverse process for rib articulation
Absence of a spinous process
Fused vertebral bodies
explanation: Thoracic vertebrae are identified by costal facets on both the body (for the rib head) and transverse process (for the rib tubercle). They also have a heart-shaped body, long spinous process, and circular vertebral foramen. Cervical vertebrae have transverse foramina. Lumbar have large bean-shaped bodies.

stem: What is the transverse ligament of the atlas and what would happen if it ruptured?
options: Connects C1 to the occipital bone — rupture causes loss of head flexion
Holds the dens of C2 against the anterior arch of C1 — rupture allows the dens to compress the spinal cord potentially fatally
Connects adjacent transverse processes — rupture causes scoliosis
Connects C1 to C2 spinous processes — rupture causes excessive rotation
Forms the posterior atlanto-axial ligament — rupture causes neck pain only
explanation: The transverse ligament of the atlas holds the odontoid process (dens) of C2 against the anterior arch of C1. It is often described as the most important ligament in the body. Rupture or fracture of the dens allows it to displace posteriorly, compressing the spinal cord at C1 — which can be fatal if it disrupts innervation to the diaphragm.

stem: What are the three groups of back muscles, what is the action of each, and what innervates the deep group?
answer: Superficial group (extrinsic) — movement of the upper limb. Intermediate group (extrinsic) — accessory muscles of respiration. Deep group (intrinsic) — posture and movements of the vertebral column. Deep muscles are innervated by the posterior rami of spinal nerves at each level.
explanation: Specific deep muscles: erector spinae extends the vertebral column / serratus posterior superior elevates ribs during inspiration. Trapezius and latissimus dorsi are superficial group muscles.

stem: What curvature is normal in the thoracic and sacral spine, and what curvature is normal in the cervical and lumbar spine?
options: Lordosis in thoracic and sacral / kyphosis in cervical and lumbar
Kyphosis in thoracic and sacral / lordosis in cervical and lumbar
Scoliosis in thoracic / lordosis in lumbar
Kyphosis throughout / lordosis in cervical only
All regions have lordosis in the healthy spine
explanation: Kyphosis (concave anteriorly) is normal in the thoracic and sacral regions — these are primary curvatures reflecting the original fetal shape. Lordosis (concave posteriorly) is normal in the cervical and lumbar regions — these are secondary curvatures that develop after birth. Pathological lateral deviation is scoliosis.

stem: What is the pulmonary ligament and what is its function?
options: A ligament anchoring the lung to the mediastinum — stabilises the inferior lobe and may accommodate lung movement during respiration
A ligament connecting the two lungs across the midline
A fold of visceral pleura connecting the lung to the diaphragm — prevents pneumothorax
A ligament anchoring the hilum to the vertebral column
A fold of parietal pleura forming the costomediastinal recess
explanation: The pulmonary ligament is a double fold of pleura extending inferiorly from the lung root to the diaphragm. It stabilises the position of the inferior lobe of the lung and may accommodate movement of the lungs during respiration. It is identified on the mediastinal surface of the lung below the hilum.

stem: Which structures are found in the posterior mediastinum?
options: Heart / pericardium / tracheal bifurcation
Thymus / internal thoracic vessels
Thoracic aorta / azygos and hemiazygos veins / oesophagus / thoracic duct / vagus nerves / sympathetic chains
Arch of aorta / brachiocephalic veins / trachea
Phrenic nerves / fibrous pericardium
explanation: Posterior mediastinum contains: thoracic (descending) aorta / azygos and hemiazygos veins / oesophagus / thoracic duct / vagus nerves / sympathetic chains. Middle mediastinum = heart / pericardium / great vessel origins / tracheal bifurcation. Superior mediastinum = arch of aorta / SVC / trachea / oesophagus / thymus.
